title: Feature request: Error correction code for the whole packet

messages.author: awhite
messages.text: Recently we've been seeing something that looks like payload corruption in packets, but with headers intact. This causes packets to be needlessly forwarded and creates "monster paths" - see https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1386 for more details. Such partially corrupted packets could be dropped early if the protocol added a field in the header (or a new trailer) to pass a few ECC bits calculated over the whole packet.
messages.content_html: Recently we've been seeing something that looks like payload corruption in packets, but with headers intact. This causes packets to be needlessly forwarded and creates "monster paths" - see https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1386 for more details. Such partially corrupted packets could be dropped early if the protocol added a field in the header (or a new trailer) to pass a few ECC bits calculated over the whole packet.
embeds_html:
Potential bug: routing loops · Issue #1386 · meshcore-dev/MeshCore
Recently I've been looking at the traffic in Warsaw, Poland area (WAW) using logs from the meshcore-bot. Currently there's over 250 repeaters in our area, with moderate traffic and with occ...

messages.author: awhite
messages.text: another point to check and quickly discard corrupted packets - in the ticket above we've observed packets with path_len=132. This should be immediately detected as a value above MAX_PATH_LEN and the packet discarded.
messages.content_html: another point to check and quickly discard corrupted packets - in the ticket above we've observed packets with path_len=132. This should be immediately detected as a value above MAX_PATH_LEN and the packet discarded.

messages.author: Beylix
messages.text: Lora does already have error correction over the transmission by itself already by using CR right? Maybe we need to stick with CR8 instead of CR5 for better error correction
messages.content_html: Lora does already have error correction over the transmission by itself already by using CR right? Maybe we need to stick with CR8 instead of CR5 for better error correction

messages.author: awhite
messages.text: we’re not sure how the errors got in, maybe it’s a custom buggy rpt or a node with a brownout - the packets are almost the same but random bits are flipped.
messages.content_html: we’re not sure how the errors got in, maybe it’s a custom buggy rpt or a node with a brownout - the packets are almost the same but random bits are flipped.

messages.author: Mike WB6KTN
messages.text: Circling back to this after looking at Lora.. The header is fixed size and sent at a code rate of 8. The header tells the receiver enough to decode the payload. Extending the header would be a massive undertaking as it needs to be a multiple of the symbol size and ... It is normal for a short header with high FEC to be received correctly while a longer payload with low FEC to fail. Though, I would have expected the payload to have failed recovery and been discarded.
messages.content_html: Circling back to this after looking at Lora.. The header is fixed size and sent at a code rate of 8. The header tells the receiver enough to decode the payload. Extending the header would be a massive undertaking as it needs to be a multiple of the symbol size and ... It is normal for a short header with high FEC to be received correctly while a longer payload with low FEC to fail. Though, I would have expected the payload to have failed recovery and been discarded.

messages.author: awhite
messages.text: ah, maybe I didn't communicate this properly .. I didn't think the errors came from the LoRa layer, rather it looks like they were introduced at the software layer (mem corruption? brownout? buggy custom rpt?). So the LoRa data was correct but the MC packet data was slightly incorrect - the payload is checksummed and it was valid, but the MC header was not and it's not checksummed.
messages.content_html: ah, maybe I didn't communicate this properly .. I didn't think the errors came from the LoRa layer, rather it looks like they were introduced at the software layer (mem corruption? brownout? buggy custom rpt?). So the LoRa data was correct but the MC packet data was slightly incorrect - the payload is checksummed and it was valid, but the MC header was not and it's not checksummed.

messages.author: Mike WB6KTN
messages.text: Seems I misunderstood. There is no protection for data corrupted before or after LoRa gets it. LoRa separately protects the header and the payload. Are you suggesting some sort of application level protection (checksum) over the entire packet? On a perhaps related topic, yes, packets that have unreasonable values should be discarded.
messages.content_html: Seems I misunderstood. There is no protection for data corrupted before or after LoRa gets it. LoRa separately protects the header and the payload. Are you suggesting some sort of application level protection (checksum) over the entire packet? On a perhaps related topic, yes, packets that have unreasonable values should be discarded.

messages.author: awhite
messages.text: I'm a belt + suspenders type of guy we should definitely verify that packets have valid and/or sane values.
messages.content_html: I'm a belt + suspenders type of guy we should definitely verify that packets have valid and/or sane values.
